features.summary: Bank of America reported Q4 2024 net income of $6.7 billion ($0.82 EPS), finishing a strong year with $102 billion in revenue and $27.1 billion in full-year net income. The company achieved growth across all revenue categories in Q4, with net interest income bottoming out in Q2 2024 at $13.9 billion and recovering to $14.5 billion by Q4. Deposit growth resumed for six consecutive quarters, while loan growth accelerated with $20 billion added in Q4. The company maintained strong credit quality with net charge-offs stable around $1.5 billion and returned $21 billion to shareholders in 2024.
features.management_outlook: Management expects record NII in 2025, projecting 6-7% growth with Q4 2025 exit rate of $15.5-15.7 billion on a fully taxable equivalent basis. They anticipate modest Q1 NII growth despite fewer calendar days, with acceleration through the year driven by continued deposit and loan growth in a 2-3% GDP environment. Expense growth is expected to moderate to 2-3% in 2025 from higher 2024 levels, while credit losses should remain stable at 50-60 basis points assuming current economic conditions persist.
features.risks: Key risks include potential volatility in interest rate expectations affecting NII projections, which assume the current forward curve materializes with only one rate cut expected in 2025. The company faces ongoing compliance costs related to an OCC consent order, though management indicates peak quarterly costs occurred in Q4 2024. Economic uncertainties include potential changes in GDP growth, employment levels, and geopolitical factors that could impact loan demand and credit quality.
features.key_drivers: Primary growth drivers include organic client acquisition with over 1 million net new checking accounts and 24,000 new wealth management households in 2024, plus continued digital engagement with 14 billion platform logins. Investment banking gained market share with 44% Q4 growth, while sales and trading achieved an 11th consecutive quarter of year-over-year growth. The company benefits from asset repricing as fixed-rate securities and loans roll off at higher rates, plus $953 billion in liquidity providing flexibility for growth and capital deployment.
